common-raven.size: About 63 cm (25 in) long; averages 1.47 kg (3.2 lb), up to 2 kg — the heaviest passerine
common-raven.habitat: Widespread across the Northern Hemisphere; forests, mountains, tundra, coasts, and areas near human habitation
common-raven.diet: Omnivore — carrion, insects, cereal grains, berries, fruit, small animals, nesting birds, and food waste
common-raven.migration: Mostly resident; mated pairs defend a territory year-round, though young birds may wander

house-finch.size: Small finch, similar in size to a sparrow
house-finch.habitat: Open woodlands, brushy desert edges, grasslands, and suburban/urban areas across North America
house-finch.migration: Largely resident year-round across most of the United States and Mexico

purple-finch.habitat: Forests and woodland edges in northern United States, southern Canada, and the Pacific coast
purple-finch.migration: Migratory; breeds in northern North America and the west coast, winters in the southern United States

house-sparrow.habitat: Strongly associated with human habitation; urban and rural settings worldwide, avoids dense forests and extreme deserts
house-sparrow.diet: Primarily seeds of grains and flowering plants; also insects, caterpillars, and other invertebrates opportunistically
house-sparrow.migration: Largely resident; one of the most widely distributed birds in the world due to human-assisted introductions

song-sparrow.habitat: Highly adaptable; found in open woodlands, marshes, brushy fields, and suburban gardens across North America
song-sparrow.migration: Partially migratory; northern populations move south in winter, southern populations largely resident

white-throated-sparrow.habitat: Boreal forests and thickets in summer; woodland edges, parks, and brush in winter
white-throated-sparrow.migration: Migratory; breeds in northern North America, winters in the southern United States

dark-eyed-junco.habitat: Coniferous and mixed forests in summer; gardens, fields, and woodland edges in winter across temperate North America
dark-eyed-junco.diet: Primarily seeds; also insects in summer
dark-eyed-junco.migration: Migratory; breeds across Canada and into the Arctic, winters widely across the United States

eastern-bluebird.habitat: Open woodlands, farmlands, and orchards in eastern North America
eastern-bluebird.diet: Insects and small invertebrates; also berries and fruit
eastern-bluebird.migration: Migratory; a migratory thrush, moving south in autumn, though some southern birds are resident

mountain-bluebird.habitat: Open mountainous areas, meadows, and rangelands in western North America
mountain-bluebird.diet: Omnivore — spiders, grasshoppers, flies, and other insects, plus small fruits
mountain-bluebird.migration: Migratory; breeds in mountainous western North America, moves to lower elevations and southern areas in winter

gray-catbird.habitat: Dense shrubby cover, forest edges, thickets, and garden hedges in North and Central America
gray-catbird.migration: Migratory; breeds across North America, winters in Central America, the Caribbean, and Gulf Coast

northern-mockingbird.habitat: Open areas, forest edges, open woodlands, and suburban/urban environments across North America and the Caribbean
northern-mockingbird.diet: Omnivore — fruit, invertebrates, and small vertebrates
northern-mockingbird.migration: Mostly resident; northern mockingbirds may move south during inclement weather or onset of winter

brown-thrasher.habitat: Shrubby areas, dense thickets, and woodland edges in eastern and central North America
brown-thrasher.diet: Omnivore — insects, fruit, nuts, and berries
brown-thrasher.migration: Migratory in the north; breeds across eastern and central North America, winters in the southern United States

cedar-waxwing.habitat: Open wooded areas, orchards, and berry-bearing shrubs across North and Central America
cedar-waxwing.diet: Cedar cones, fruit, holly berries, and insects
cedar-waxwing.migration: Migratory; breeds in open wooded areas of southern Canada, winters in the southern United States, Central America, and northwestern South America

tufted-titmouse.habitat: Deciduous and mixed forests, woodland edges, and suburban gardens in eastern North America
tufted-titmouse.migration: Largely resident in eastern North America year-round

black-capped-chickadee.habitat: Deciduous and mixed forests across northern North America, from Alaska and Canada to the northern United States
black-capped-chickadee.diet: Primarily insects and seeds; caches food for winter use
black-capped-chickadee.migration: Non-migratory; resident year-round across its range, with some local movements in winter

carolina-chickadee.size: Small passerine, slightly smaller than the black-capped chickadee
carolina-chickadee.habitat: Forests, woodland edges, and suburban parks in the southern United States
carolina-chickadee.migration: Largely resident in the southeastern United States year-round

white-breasted-nuthatch.size: About 15.5 cm (6.1 in) long — medium-sized nuthatch
white-breasted-nuthatch.habitat: Deciduous forests and mixed woodlands at low elevations across much of North America
white-breasted-nuthatch.diet: Insects and arthropods in summer; seeds in winter
white-breasted-nuthatch.migration: Largely resident; breeds throughout much of North America and generally stays year-round

red-breasted-nuthatch.habitat: Coniferous forests across Canada, Alaska, and the northeastern and western United States
red-breasted-nuthatch.diet: Mainly insects and seeds, especially conifer seeds
red-breasted-nuthatch.migration: Partially resident; regularly irrupts south when cone crops fail, with records as far south as the Gulf Coast

brown-creeper.habitat: Mature forests, particularly with large trees, across North America
brown-creeper.diet: Insects and spiders gleaned from bark crevices
brown-creeper.migration: Partially migratory; northern populations move south in winter, southern populations largely resident

house-wren.habitat: Suburban areas, forest edges, gardens, and brushy thickets in southern Canada, the USA, and Mexico
house-wren.diet: Primarily insects found in crevices and dense cover
house-wren.migration: Migratory; breeds across North America, moves south to warmer regions in winter

carolina-wren.habitat: Dense forest cover, farm edges, and suburban thickets in the eastern United States and extreme southern Ontario
carolina-wren.migration: Largely resident; severe winters restrict northern range but birds generally remain year-round

ruby-crowned-kinglet.size: One of the smallest songbirds in North America
ruby-crowned-kinglet.habitat: Spruce-fir forests in northern and mountainous regions of the US and Canada in summer; varied habitats in winter
ruby-crowned-kinglet.diet: Mainly insects; also fruits and seeds
ruby-crowned-kinglet.migration: Mostly migratory; breeds in northern Canada and Alaska, winters south to Mexico

golden-crowned-kinglet.habitat: Coniferous and mixed forests throughout much of North America
golden-crowned-kinglet.migration: Partially migratory; northern populations move south in winter, many remain in coniferous forests year-round

yellow-warbler.habitat: Woodland edges, thickets, willows, and open areas near water across most of North America
yellow-warbler.migration: Migratory; breeds across most of North America, winters in Central and South America

common-yellowthroat.habitat: Marshes, wetland edges, brushy fields, and dense low vegetation across North and Central America
common-yellowthroat.migration: Migratory in the north; winters in southern parts of the breeding range, Central America, and the West Indies; southern forms largely resident

american-redstart.habitat: Deciduous and mixed woodlands across North America
american-redstart.diet: Insects gleaned from foliage and caught in flight
american-redstart.migration: Migratory; breeds in North America, winters in Central America, the Caribbean, and northern South America

indigo-bunting.size: 11.5–13 cm (4.5–5.1 in) long — small seed-eating bird
indigo-bunting.habitat: Farmland, brush areas, and open woodland from southern Canada to northern Florida
indigo-bunting.diet: Primarily insects in summer; seeds in winter
indigo-bunting.migration: Migratory; breeds from southern Canada to northern Florida, winters from southern Florida to northern South America; migrates by night using stars

rose-breasted-grosbeak.habitat: Cool-temperate open deciduous woods across much of eastern North America
rose-breasted-grosbeak.diet: Seeds (foliage gleaner); also insects and berries
rose-breasted-grosbeak.migration: Migratory; breeds in eastern North America, winters in tropical America

scarlet-tanager.habitat: Thick deciduous woodlands and suburbs across eastern North America
scarlet-tanager.migration: Migratory; breeds in eastern North America, winters in South America

baltimore-oriole.habitat: Open woodlands, forest edges, and suburban areas in eastern North America
baltimore-oriole.migration: Migratory; breeds in eastern North America, winters in Central America and northern South America

red-winged-blackbird.habitat: Marshes, wetlands, fields, and roadsides across most of North and Central America
red-winged-blackbird.migration: Migratory in the north; northern populations move south to Mexico and the southern United States in winter, some may winter as far north as Pennsylvania and British Columbia

common-grackle.size: Medium-to-large icterid with a long tail
common-grackle.habitat: Highly adaptable; open areas, agricultural fields, suburbs, and woodland edges east of the Rocky Mountains
common-grackle.diet: Omnivore — grains, insects, small vertebrates, and refuse
common-grackle.migration: Partially migratory; northern populations move south in winter, many are resident

brown-headed-cowbird.habitat: Open woodlands, farmland, and suburban areas across temperate and subtropical North America
brown-headed-cowbird.diet: Seeds and insects; historically followed bison herds for disturbed-ground insects
brown-headed-cowbird.migration: Partially migratory; permanent resident in the south, northern birds migrate to the southern United States and Mexico in winter

eastern-meadowlark.habitat: Open grasslands, meadows, and agricultural fields from eastern North America to northern South America
eastern-meadowlark.diet: Insects and other invertebrates; also seeds and grain
eastern-meadowlark.migration: Partially migratory; northern populations move south in winter, many southern birds are resident

bobolink.habitat: Open grasslands and hay fields in the northern United States and Canada during breeding
bobolink.diet: Seeds (cultivated grains during migration and winter) and insects in summer
bobolink.migration: Long-distance migrant; breeds in northern US and Canada, winters in southern South America (Paraguay, Argentina, Bolivia)

blue-grosbeak.habitat: Brushy fields, woodland edges, roadsides, and open areas in the southern United States and northern Mexico
blue-grosbeak.migration: Migratory; breeds in the southern United States and northern Mexico, winters in Central America

eastern-towhee.habitat: Brushy areas, shrubby undergrowth, and woodland edges across eastern North America
eastern-towhee.diet: Seeds, insects, and berries scratched from the leaf litter
eastern-towhee.migration: Partially migratory; northern birds migrate to the southern United States in winter

chipping-sparrow.habitat: Open woodlands, forest edges, suburbs, and parks across North America
chipping-sparrow.migration: Partial migrant; northerly populations fly south to overwinter in Mexico and the southern United States

tree-swallow.habitat: Open areas near water, woodland edges, and fields across the United States and Canada; nests in tree cavities
tree-swallow.diet: Primarily flying insects; also mollusks, spiders, and fruit
tree-swallow.migration: Migratory; breeds in the US and Canada, winters along southern US coasts south to Panama and the West Indies

purple-martin.habitat: Open areas near water, suburbs, and areas with martin nest boxes across eastern North America and parts of the Pacific coast
purple-martin.diet: Flying insects caught in aerial pursuit
purple-martin.migration: Migratory; breeds in North America, winters in South America; makes a brief stopover in the Yucatán or Cuba

eastern-phoebe.habitat: Woodland edges, stream banks, farms, and areas near bridges or buildings in eastern North America
eastern-phoebe.diet: Mainly flying insects caught by sallying from a perch
eastern-phoebe.migration: Migratory; one of the earliest spring migrants in eastern North America, winters in the southeastern US and Central America

eastern-kingbird.habitat: Open areas with scattered trees and bushes across North America
eastern-kingbird.diet: Primarily insects; also fruit during migration and winter
eastern-kingbird.migration: Migratory; breeds across North America, winters in Central and South America

great-crested-flycatcher.habitat: Treetops in eastern and mid-western deciduous forests across North America
great-crested-flycatcher.diet: Insects caught in the canopy; also fruit and berries
great-crested-flycatcher.migration: Migratory; breeds in eastern and mid-western North America, winters in Central and South America

downy-woodpecker.size: 14–18 cm (5.5–7.1 in) long — the smallest woodpecker in North America
downy-woodpecker.habitat: Forested areas throughout the United States and Canada, except deserts and northern tundra
downy-woodpecker.diet: Primarily insects; supplements diet with seeds and berries
downy-woodpecker.migration: Largely resident year-round across its North American range

hairy-woodpecker.size: About 250 mm (9.8 in) long; wingspan about 380 mm (15 in) — medium-sized woodpecker
hairy-woodpecker.habitat: Forests across a large area of North America
hairy-woodpecker.diet: Insects excavated from wood; also seeds and berries
hairy-woodpecker.migration: Largely resident year-round across North America

northern-flicker.habitat: Open forests, forest edges, and suburban areas across most of North America, parts of Central America, and Cuba
northern-flicker.diet: Ants and beetles from the ground; also berries and seeds
northern-flicker.migration: One of the few migratory woodpeckers; northern populations move south in winter

pileated-woodpecker.size: Large, crow-sized woodpecker — the largest extant woodpecker in North America
pileated-woodpecker.habitat: Mature deciduous forests in eastern North America, the Great Lakes, boreal forests of Canada, and parts of the Pacific Coast
pileated-woodpecker.diet: Primarily insects (especially carpenter ants and beetle larvae) extracted from dead wood
pileated-woodpecker.migration: Largely resident; non-migratory in most of its range

red-bellied-woodpecker.habitat: Deciduous woodlands and forest edges in the eastern United States, from Canada south to Florida
red-bellied-woodpecker.migration: Largely resident; breeds and remains year-round in the eastern United States

red-headed-woodpecker.habitat: Open country, open forests, and woodland edges across southern Canada and the east-central United States
red-headed-woodpecker.diet: Insects, nuts, seeds, berries, and occasionally small rodents or bird eggs
red-headed-woodpecker.migration: Partially migratory; some populations move south in winter, others are resident

belted-kingfisher.habitat: Near water — rivers, lakes, ponds, estuaries, and coastlines across North America
belted-kingfisher.diet: Primarily fish, plus crustaceans, frogs, and small reptiles
belted-kingfisher.migration: Partially migratory; northern birds move south in winter; many are resident near open water year-round

chimney-swift.habitat: Aerial forager over towns, cities, and forests; roosts and nests in chimneys and hollow trees
chimney-swift.migration: Long-distance migrant; spends winters in South America, returns to North America to breed

mourning-dove.size: Medium-sized dove — one of North America's most abundant birds
mourning-dove.habitat: Open fields, agricultural land, woodland edges, and suburban/urban areas across North America
mourning-dove.diet: Almost exclusively seeds; young fed crop milk by parents
mourning-dove.migration: Mostly resident; capable of speeds up to 88 km/h (55 mph), some northern birds move south in winter

rock-pigeon.habitat: Cliff faces, rock ledges, and urban/suburban environments worldwide; open and semi-open areas for foraging
rock-pigeon.migration: Largely resident; urban and feral populations are non-migratory

killdeer.habitat: Open fields with short vegetation, rooftops, coastal wetlands, beaches, and agricultural areas across the Americas
killdeer.diet: Primarily insects; also other invertebrates and seeds, foraging almost exclusively in fields
killdeer.migration: Partially migratory; North American breeders winter from the resident range south to Central America and the West Indies; Caribbean and South American subspecies are resident

american-woodcock.habitat: Brushy, young-forest upland habitats in the eastern half of North America; spends time on the ground in dense cover
american-woodcock.diet: Earthworms and other soil invertebrates probed from moist ground
american-woodcock.migration: Migratory; breeds in the eastern half of North America, moves south in winter

spotted-sandpiper.habitat: Freshwater and brackish shorelines — streams, rivers, lakes, and ponds across North America
spotted-sandpiper.diet: Insects, worms, small fish, and crustaceans
spotted-sandpiper.migration: Migratory; breeds across North America, winters from the southern United States to South America

greater-yellowlegs.habitat: Boreal forest bogs and wetlands in summer; mudflats, marshes, and shallow wetlands during migration and winter
greater-yellowlegs.diet: Small fish, invertebrates, and insects probed from shallow water
greater-yellowlegs.migration: Migratory; breeds in central Canada and southern Alaska, winters in southern North America, Central America, and South America

sanderling.size: Small wading bird, similar in size to a dunlin but stouter
sanderling.habitat: Arctic tundra in summer; sandy beaches and coastal mudflats worldwide in winter
sanderling.diet: Small crabs, invertebrates, and insects; horseshoe crab eggs during spring migration stopover
sanderling.migration: Long-distance migrant; breeds in the High Arctic, winters south to South America, Africa, Europe, and Australia

ruddy-turnstone.habitat: Rocky and sandy coastlines, mudflats, and beaches worldwide in winter; Arctic tundra in summer
ruddy-turnstone.diet: Invertebrates, mollusks, crustaceans, and carrion — flips stones and debris to uncover prey
ruddy-turnstone.migration: Highly migratory; breeds in northern Eurasia and North America, winters on coastlines almost worldwide

american-avocet.habitat: Shallow wetlands, mudflats, alkaline lakes, and flooded fields in North America
american-avocet.diet: Crustaceans and insects swept from shallow water by sweeping the bill side to side
american-avocet.migration: Migratory; breeds in western North America, winters along the Gulf Coast and in Central America
